title: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined

input_row.title: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined
input_row.description: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
input_row.rationale: Running different applications on the same Kubernetes cluster creates a risk of one compromised application attacking a neighboring application. Network segmentation is important to ensure that containers can communicate only with those they are supposed to. A network policy is a specification of how selections of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Once there is any Network Policy in a namespace selecting a particular pod, that pod will reject any connections that are not allowed by any Network Policy. Other pods in the namespace that are not selected by any Network Policy will continue to accept all traffic"
input_row.audit: Run the below command and review the NetworkPolicy objects created in the cluster. kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces Ensure that each namespace defined in the cluster has at least one Network Policy.
input_row.remediation: Follow the documentation and create NetworkPolicy objects as you need them.
input_row.impact: Once there is any Network Policy in a namespace selecting a particular pod, that pod will reject any connections that are not allowed by any Network Policy. Other pods in the namespace that are not selected by any Network Policy will continue to accept all traffic"
input_row.default_value: By default, network policies are not created.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit control is well-defined and clearly mentions using commands to verify the property, lending itself to automation.
- The control deals with Network Policies in a Kubernetes environment, but AWS does not directly provide a service equivalent to Kubernetes Network Policies in the same way Azure AKS does. AWS manages container orchestrations through services like EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service).
- While the specific check for Network Policies is less straightforward in AWS than in Azure, the compliance control aims at network segmentation and isolation. We can use AWS APIs to ensure there are security measures similar to network policies at the AWS resource level (like security groups attached to EC2 instances in EKS clusters).
- There is also the possibility to check for compliance with Security Groups and potentially implement segmentation and filtering that approximates the intent of Network Policies.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_eks_cluster_network_policy_configuration

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the following AWS API calls and boto3 SDK methods:
  - For EKS clusters: Use `boto3.eks.list_clusters()` to enumerate all EKS clusters.
  - For each EKS cluster, use `boto3.eks.describe_cluster()` to retrieve configuration details.
  - Validate that Security Groups associated with nodes in the EKS cluster restrict traffic appropriately akin to Network Policies.
  
- Validate Security Groups:
  - Use `boto3.ec2.describe_security_groups()` to list security groups.
  - Ensure the security groups allow only the required inbound/outbound rules for applications running within different namespaces mimicking Network Policy behavior.

- Expected configuration properties/settings to validate:
  - Each EKS cluster should have security groups that enforce traffic restrictions between different applications running on the same cluster. 

- Clear Pass/Fail criteria:
  - Pass: Security groups configured on EKS instances allow traffic only as specified and deny any unauthorized traffic.
  - Fail: Security groups configured on EKS instances allow broader access than intended, thereby failing the segmentation criteria.

This approach maintains the security mindset that Kubernetes Network Policies would typically provide in an Azure context, applied across the AWS landscape.
